coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescence assay based on self-doping biomass carbon dots for rapid detection of dimethoate
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Haochi
Ding, Jie
Chen, Ligang
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: A novel approach was proposed to prepare carbon dots (CDs) by acid carbonization and hydrothermal treatment of pork rib bones from food waste. The synthesized animal waste biomass CDs have the merits of eco-friendly, superior fluorescence properties and biocompatibility. The fluorescence of animal waste biomass CDs could be quenched efficiently by dithizone due to the coordination effect. With the addition of dimethoate, the dithizone ligands at CDs are replaced by hydrolysates, and hence the fluorescence is recovered. Thus, a sensing platform was constructed based on the coordination-originated fluorescence of animal waste biomass CDs which can sensitively and selectively detect dimethoate due to a simple displacement mechanism. Upon optimal conditions, the detection limit of dimethoate is 0.064 μM. The linear ranges were obtained in a range of 0.15 μM–5.0 μM. The method is rapid, convenient and environmentally acceptable that can directly detect dimethoate in water samples with recoveries of 93 %–105 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 2 %–6 %, respectively. This simple strategy can be used to develop the fluorescence recovery sensors for chemical detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Animal waste biomass carbon dots
Dimethoate
Fluorescent probe
Dithizone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dithizone-functionalized CDs fluorescence probe and detection of dimethoate
Absorbance, excitation-dependent emission characteristic of CDs
The effect of pH on CDs
The effect of solvents on CDs
Dithizone-functionalized CDs with dimethoate at different concentrations
Practical application in environmental water and the comparison with other methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A, insert: HRTEM of CDs), FT-IR spectra of CDs (B), XRD pattern of CDs (C), and TG, DTG of CDs (D).
XPS full survey (A), High-resolution XPS of C1s (B), S2p (C), O1s (D), Ca2p (E) and N1s (F) spectra of CDs.

UV spectrum of CDs (A). Fluorescence spectra of CDs excited at different wavelengths (300-400 nm) (B). The photostability of CDs (C). The salt effects of CDs (D). Effect of pH on CDs fluorescence intensity (E). Fluorescence spectra of CDs dispersed in different solvents (F).

Effect of equilibrium time on fluorescence intensity of CDs with dithizone (10 mg L−1) (A). The influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (F0) and dithizone-functionalized CDs (F1) (B). Fluorescence spectra of CDs with dithizone (C). Logarithmic relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs with dithizone concentration (D).

Effect of equilibrium time on fluorescence intensity of dithizone-functionalized CDs with dimethoate (2 μM) (A). Fluorescence spectra of dithizone-functionalized CDs system with dimethoate (B). Stern-Volmer plots from dithizone-functionalized CDs with dimethoate concentration (C).

UV absorption spectra of CDs, dithizone and dithizone-functionalized CDs (A). Fluorescence decay profile of CDs (B) and CDs with dithizone (C). The fluorescence switch mechanism for dimethoate detection (D).
Comparison with other methods for dimethoate detection.

row.entry: Tap water, green tea, apple juice

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescence assay based on self-doping biomass carbon dots for rapid detection of dimethoate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource, Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: c College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource, Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence switch mechanism for dimethoate detection.

A novel approach was proposed to prepare carbon dots (CDs) by acid carbonization and hydrothermal treatment of pork rib bones from food waste. The synthesized animal waste biomass CDs have the merits of eco-friendly, superior fluorescence properties and biocompatibility. The fluorescence of animal waste biomass CDs could be quenched efficiently by dithizone due to the coordination effect. With the addition of dimethoate, the dithizone ligands at CDs are replaced by hydrolysates, and hence the fluorescence is recovered. Thus, a sensing platform was constructed based on the coordination-originated fluorescence of animal waste biomass CDs which can sensitively and selectively detect dimethoate due to a simple displacement mechanism. Upon optimal conditions, the detection limit of dimethoate is 0.064 μM. The linear ranges were obtained in a range of 0.15 μM–5.0 μM. The method is rapid, convenient and environmentally acceptable that can directly detect dimethoate in water samples with recoveries of 93 %–105 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 2 %–6 %, respectively. This simple strategy can be used to develop the fluorescence recovery sensors for chemical detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides are widely applied to control plant diseases and insects in the past few decades. However, the excessive use of organophosphorus pesticides will cause widespread pollution of soil, water and agricultural products, which finally result in long-term accumulation in the ecosystem. [1,2] Dimethoate, as a common organophosphorus pesticide, was used to control diseases and insect pests in crops [3]. Due to the extensive use of dimethoate pesticides, the residual amount in food and water is increasing. The harm to humans has been reported in many cases, such as depression, anxiety, even regenerative disorders and cancer [4]. So, it is of significance to provide an efficient and simple method for dimethoate detection to protect the ecosystem.

With the development and application of new technologies, several methods were used to detect dimethoate including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), [5] gas chromatography [6], thin layer chromatography [7], electrochemical sensor [8], enzyme-linked immunoassay and biosensor [9]. Most of these methods meet the requirements for trace analysis of pesticide residues with high precision and sensitivity. However, most of them have the disadvantages of complicated operation, time-consuming, laborious, and high cost. For instance, HPLC has high sensitivity and selectivity. But the limitation is tedious operating procedure and high cost. Gas chromatography has been the most common method for detecting organophosphorus pesticides in the environment, but this method involves many complicated pretreatment processes such as sample extraction, purification, and concentration, resulting in slow monitoring speed, and poor continuity. The disadvantage of electrochemical sensors is the poor reproducibility.

In order to overcome these shortcomings, carbon nanodots have become the most attractive candidates to prepare photoluminescent materials because of their strong luminescence, easy preparation, and resistance to photobleaching. [10,11] Biomass carbon dots (CDs) as a kind of carbon nanodots are an indispensable part in the fluorescent nanoparticles development. The CDs prepared from biomass materials have advantages of eco-friendly, low cost and inexhaustible supplying [12,13], which have broad application prospects in the detection of small molecules [14], metal ions [15], and biomolecules [16]. However, most of CDs based fluorescence probe are mainly according to fluorescence quenching [17,18], which lead to low selectivity of the method. Fluorescence recovery strategy is rare than fluorescence quenching strategy. But fluorescence recovery assay often has better selectivity and sensitivity and can achieve the determination of specific substances [19].

In this work, an efficient and low-cost strategy is proposed to prepare biomass CDs by acid carbonization of pork rib bones assisted with hydrothermal method. Calcium, sulfur and nitrogen elements are doped in CDs and higher reaction temperature is beneficial to improving the fluorescence property. We have established a fluorescent switch process, which functionalized animal waste biomass CDs, and used the fluorescence recovery patterns to achieve selective detection of dimethoate pesticides. When the animal waste biomass CDs were mixed with dithizone, the fluorescence was quenched owing to coordination effect. After adding dimethoate into the system, dithizone was separated from the synthesized CDs, and the fluorescence is restored. Herein, we demonstrate a simple fluorescence assay to selectively and sensitively detect dimethoate without using expensive enzymes or antibodies.

Dithizone, dimethoate and dimercaprol were acquired from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Nitric acid, sulfuric acid, ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, acetone and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Kermel (Tianjin, China). Pork rib bones were obtained from food waste.

The morphological of CDs was carried out by an H-7650 TEM (Hitachi, Japan). The XRD spectrum was obtained by an XRD-6100 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The elemental and structure analysis of CDs was carried out using an ESCALAB250 electron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA) and an FT-IR360 spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). The fluorescence spectra and UV spectra were recorded by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a TU-1901 spectrometer (PERSEE, China). The thermogravimetry analysis (TG) at 45−800 °C was carried out on a Pyris 1 TGA Instrument (MA, USA).

Firstly, the pork rib bones were washed using deionized water to remove flesh and other impurities, followed by drying at 60 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, the dried pork rib bones were pulverized into small pieces by grinder. Afterward, a sample of 3.0 g pork rib bones was heated in a muffle furnace at 700 °C for 5 h. Then, the carbonized product was ground into powder and 100 mg of the above bone ash was dispersed in water (3 mL) and H2SO4 (10 mL, 95–98 %), and then ultrasonicated for 20 min. Subsequently, 20 mL of HNO3 (68–70 %) was added and ultrasonicated for 20 min. After acid oxidation treatment, the dispersion was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 6 min, then washed to neutral. The obtained sample in water (30 mL) was added into a Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 10 h. The obtained solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dithizone-functionalized CDs fluorescence probe and detection of dimethoate
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the quenching effect of dithizone on animal waste biomass CDs, a series of dithizone concentrations (200 μL) and 100 μL of animal waste biomass CDs were mixed well, adjusted to pH = 11 by NaOH solution, and finally diluted to 1 mL. The mixture was incubated for 3 min, so that the dithizone and CDs were completely combined in the strongly alkaline media. Such a dithizone-functionalized CDs probe was prepared and can be utilized to detect dimethoate.

For the detection of dimethoate, a series of different dimethoate concentrations (200 μL) were added into dithizone-functionalized CDs solution, then finally diluted to 1 mL. After 15 min to mix thoroughly, the fluorescent signal was recorded on an excitation wavelength at 315 nm. Fluorescence measurement parameters such as slit widths (10 nm), excitation voltage (750 V), and scanning speed (240 nm min−1) were set.

The river water and farm water were collected from Harbin (China). The samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The recoveries were studied by adding a certain concentration of dimethoate to practical water samples.

In this work, pork rib bones were used to prepare biomass CDs by acid treatment assisted with hydrothermal method. First, pork rib bones were treated at 700 °C for 5 h and converted to bone ash. The bone ash was acid carbonization and hydrothermally treated, which generated animal waste biomass CDs. Chemical oxidants (H2SO4/HNO3) were used to facilitate the carbonization of pork rib bones to synthetize CDs. Moreover, during the preparation process, the oxygen-containing functional groups were introduced which makes CDs have excellent hydrophilicity and be easy to modify. [20] The yield of animal waste biomass CDs was 14.9 wt% which was calculated by the weight of bone ash.

Presumably, the temperature and reaction time can affect the formation and fluorescence characteristics of CDs. A preliminary attempt was made to study the effect of reaction temperatures from 140 °C to 220 °C on the fluorescence intensity. As reaction temperature was increased, the fluorescence intensity raised gradually. We held the view that low temperatures may not be conducive to the carbonization and surface functionalization of animal waste biomass CDs. [21] When the temperature was over 200 °C, it exceeded the reactor tolerance and increased the dangerous. In addition, the effect of reaction time (6 h, 8 h, 10 h, 12 h) was also investigated. As the reaction time extended from 6 h to 10 h, the fluorescence intensity of animal waste biomass CDs was increased significantly. Then the fluorescence intensity kept stable as the reaction time exceeded 10 h. As a result, the preparation process was optimized to be heated at 200 ℃ for 10 h.

The TEM was employed to directly observe the microstructure of CDs, and the image obtained is shown in Fig. 1 A. After acid treatment and hydrothermal reaction, pork rib bones used as carbon precursors were transformed into CDs with the diameter of 4.2 ± 1.2 nm. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1A) indicated that animal waste biomass CDs had well-resolved lattice fringes with 0.22 nm spacing, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene. [22]

The characteristic bands of CDs were characterized by FT-IR. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the CDs derived from pork rib bones exhibited the existence of NH and OH stretching vibrations because of a characteristic peak at 3560 cm−1. The carboxyl of CDs has been proved to exist through the absorption band at 3230 cm−1. A small peak at 2360 cm−1 was owing to SH and CN groups. The peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 show the existence of vibrational stretching of CC and CN. [23] A peak at 1150 cm−1 is assigned to the CO, C–S and CN groups. [24]

To demonstrate the obtained CDs structure, the XRD pattern was exhibited in Fig. 1C. In case of common CDs, the XRD spectra often show a wide peak at 2θ range of 20°-25° which indicated the existence of amorphous disordered structure. However, the XRD pattern of animal waste biomass CDs had main peaks at 2θ = 11.7°, 20.8°, 23.4°, 29.1°, 31.1° and 33.4°. The phenomenon indicated that a well-ordered highly crystalline structure of CDs was formed, supporting the aforementioned TEM results. On the other hand, several less intensity peaks were also detected, indicating the ordered structure of animal waste biomass CDs. We speculated that the existence of nitrogen promotes to form polycyclic fragments in the structure of the obtained CDs. [22,25]In addition, elemental doping can be contributed to promote the structural order in CDs structures and cause high macro-crystallinity of CDs.[[25]

To further verify the thermal stability of the prepared CDs, TG and derivative TG curves of CDs were shown in Fig. 1D. Firstly, the weight loss was 1.80 % at 130 °C because of water molecules loss. Subsequently, second weight loss of CDs at 130−700 °C (25.71 % decrease) is due to organic functional groups decomposition including CO, CH, SH and OH. [26] After that, the weight did not change. The animal waste biomass CDs finally lose 27.51 %, owing to thermal decomposition of functional groups and oxygenated species loss. The results show that the animal waste biomass CDs have good thermal stability.

The elemental analysis of CDs was performed as shown in Fig. 2 A, which reveals sulfur, carbon, calcium, nitrogen, and oxygen elements. In the C1s XPS spectrum of the obtained CDs (Fig. 2B), there are four peaks at 284.3, 284.8, 285.3 and 285.8 eV, indicating the presence of CC/C = C, C–S, CN, and CO. The S2p spectrum in Fig. 2C mainly consists of two peaks centered at 169.0 and 170.1 eV, which agree with -C–SO3- and -C–SO4- species. Three peaks of O1s spectrum in Fig. 2D at 531.7, 533.1, and 532.4 eV were ascribed to CO, COC and COH bonds. There are two fitted peaks at 348.2 eV and 351.8 eV in Ca2p spectrum (Fig. 2E), which are attributed to Ca2p3/2 and Ca2p1/2, respectively. The content of calcium element is calculated to be 5.35 %. Fig. 2F shows the presence of C-N-C (400.9 eV), N-(C)3 (401.8 eV), and NH (402.5 eV). [27] The N and S contained in the animal waste biomass CDs may come from the precursor and acid [28]. The results showed that oxygen-containing functional groups are immobilized on animal waste biomass CDs, terminating the free bonds at the edges. The oxygen-containing group with high polarity makes the CDs hydrophilic in alkaline environment, thus forming stronger attraction and dissolved oxygen with the electrolyte [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance, excitation-dependent emission characteristic of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV-vis absorption spectrum was measured to discuss the fluorescence properties of CDs. The UV–vis absorption peak appeared at 259 nm, which is derived from π-π* transition of CC (Fig. 3 A). [30] The particular fluorescence properties of animal waste biomass CDs were displayed in Fig. 3B. As the excitation wavelength of the obtained CDs ranged from 300 to 400 nm, the emission peak position at 453 nm was basically unchanged because of the excitation-independent fluorescence behavior. As we known, the excitation-dependent emission characteristic of common CDs was mainly caused by different particle sizes and surface states. [31] The TEM image in this paper displays that the size of animal waste biomass CDs is 4.2 ± 1.2 nm possessing different particle sizes. Hence, the fluorescence properties of animal waste biomass CDs with excitation independence depend on surface states instead of morphology. The new energy levels in band gap were induced by surface trapping states that can result in excitation-independent of animal waste biomass CDs. We speculated that the surface traps of CDs were passivated by functional groups such as CO, CO, N-Ca-N, CN and so on. The fluorescence emission may be caused by radiative transition of sp2 carbon. A single recombination pattern resulted in the excitation-independent emission characteristic. [32] It is a conceivable explanation for the excitation-independent optical behavior.

The good stability of CDs was investigated in many aspects. The fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs did not change obviously after storage for one month. Furthermore, the influence of photostability on animal waste biomass CDs should be considered. The fluorescence intensity kept stable under UV light for 1.5 h as shown in Fig. 3C. To verify the influence of ionic strength on CDs, the fluorescence intensity was investigated at different NaCl concentrations. Fig. 3D depicts that the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged even in the existence of 0.5 M NaCl. It indicates that animal waste biomass CDs have superior stability under rigorous ionic strength conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of pH on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the CDs optical properties was studied. Fig. 3E displayed the change in fluorescence intensity with pH ranging from 3.00–12.00. The fluorescence intensity of the obtained CDs almost kept stable in the range of 3−10. Then, the fluorescence intensity continued to increase under the condition of pH = 10−12. Generally speaking, the fluorescence behaviors of common CDs are easily affected by extreme conditions that lead to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. However, the animal waste biomass CDs are able to apply under strong alkaline conditions, which have good stability and strong fluorescence intensity. In alkaline condition, the animal waste biomass CDs can form stable conjugated structures which are consist of functional groups and sp2 carbon clusters, thus achieving superior stability. [27] When animal waste biomass CDs solution is under other pH conditions, the fluorescence intensity decreased and changed the fluorescence behavior due to the change or destruction of conjugated structures. It is speculated that when the pH of solution is regulated to alkaline, the conjugated structure of animal waste biomass CDs changes to some extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of solvents on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from the effect of pH, we also investigated the fluorescent properties upon various solvents conditions. The fluorescence intensities of the synthesized CDs dispersed in water, ethanol, methanol, acetone, and acetonitrile were measured. Generally, the influence of solvents on CDs fluorescence depends on dielectric constant, refractive index as well as coordination ability. Likewise, the fluorescence property of CDs in different solvents is different. The CDs powder obtained by rotary evaporation method has superior dispersion in water, ethanol, methanol, acetone and acetonitrile, revealing the existence of polar groups. The fluorescence intensity of CDs using these solvents was in the order of water > methanol > ethanol > acetonitrile > acetone with a slight shift in their emission peak which may be because of the difference in their interaction with solvents (Fig. 3F). It should be noted that the polarity of these solvents was in the order of water > methanol > acetonitrile > acetone > ethanol. The polar aprotic solvents (acetonitrile and acetone) cannot be used as hydrogen donors, while polar protic solvents (water, ethanol and methanol) were able to become hydrogen donors. This relative change of fluorescence intensity in different solvents indicates that there may be hydrogen bonding between solvents and CDs. In addition, the higher fluorescence intensity of CDs in alcohol solvents (ethanol, methanol) has been happened. The result indicated that there is a preferential hydrogen bonding interaction between OH groups of alcoholic solvents and CO groups of CDs compared with in other non-alcholic solvents like acetonitrile and acetone. [33]

In the experiment, we studied the fluorescence response of animal waste biomass CDs for dithizone. The fluorescent intensity of CDs decreased rapidly after adding dithizone, then kept steady after 3 min (Fig. 4 A). Herein, equilibrium time was set as 3 min. The pH of system was also an important factor on the fluorescence properties. The influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs and dithizone-functionalized CDs was investigated. The pH values were ranged from 3 to 12. As seen in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased from 3 to 11 and almost kept steady at pH 11−12. The quenching efficiency achieved a maximum at pH 11. Therefore, the experiment condition was selected at the optimal pH of 11.

In the reaction system, dithizone was bonded to CDs due to coordination effect. The fluorescence intensity of system decreased remarkably as dithizone concentration increased (Fig. 4C). A linear correlation is observed between log(F0/F) and dithizone concentration as shown in Fig. 4D. The phenomenon is described as follows: log(F0/F) = 0.0266C + 0.0137 (R2 = 0.996), where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of animal waste biomass CDs in the absence and presence of dithizone, while C means the dithizone concentration. When using dithizone-functionalized CDs probe to detect dimethoate, attachment of higher dithizone concentration can cause higher quenching efficiency and introduced more dimethoate, which resulted in wide linear range and good sensitivity. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased after adding dithizone, and the fluorescence was almost completely quenched at 60 mg L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dithizone-functionalized CDs with dimethoate at different concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of dimethoate can be affected by equilibrium time. Fig. 5 A indicated that the system fluorescence was gradually restored and reached equilibrium after 15 min with the addition of dimethoate. So, 15 min was chosen as equilibrium time. Fig. 5B displayed the fluorescence intensity gradually increased as the dimethoate concentration increased. A linear correlation is observed between F/F0 and dimethoate concentration as shown in Fig. 5C. It can be described as follows: F/F0 = 0.5988c+1.0135 (R2 = 0.997). F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of dithizone-functionalized CDs in the absence and presence of dimethoate, while c represents the dimethoate concentration. The linear range was found to be 0.15−5.0 μM and the calculated limit of detection was 0.064 μM (3σ/slope), where σ means the deviation of the blank measurement (n = 11).

Fluorescence quenching generally occurs in two forms: dynamic or static quenching. To study the fluorescence quenching mechanism, UV absorption and fluorescence lifetime were measured. The binding state between animal waste biomass CDs and dithizone can be determined by the change of UV absorption spectrum. Fig. 6 A shows the changes in the UV spectrum of system after adding dithizone. It was found that the formation of ground-state complexes would change the absorption spectrum of animal waste biomass CDs. When the CDs were mixed with dithizone, the absorbance peak of animal waste biomass CDs at 259 nm and the absorbance peak of dithizone at 251 nm disappeared and a new absorbance peak appeared at 247 nm. The UV spectra proved that animal waste biomass CDs have obvious complexation with dithizone. We speculated that the dithizone can connect with calcium in animal waste biomass CDs through a coordinating reaction and form dithizone-functionalized CDs complex. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism belongs to static quenching.

In addition, we also tested the fluorescence lifetime. Dynamic quenching occurred along with the change of fluorescence lifetime, while static quenching is the opposite. The fluorescence decay curves of CDs in the absence and presence of dithizone were investigated to further study the mechanism. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was 3.86 ns as shown in Fig. 6B. After adding dithizone, the average fluorescence lifetime changed to 2.64 ns (Fig. 6C). The change in fluorescence lifetime after adding dithizone indicates the existence of dynamic quenching. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of animal waste biomass CDs is a coexistence process of dynamic quenching and static quenching. [34,35]

Upon the addition of dimethoate into the fluorescence probe, it prevented the interaction between dithizone and animal waste biomass CDs. Dimethoate can be hydrolyzed to generate (a) and (b) under alkaline conditions (Fig. S1), The (a) part with a PS bond replaces the dithizone ligands on the animal waste biomass CDs owing to its stronger binding preference toward calcium than dithizone. Thus, dithizone can be separated from animal waste biomass CDs. Fig. 6D depicts the fluorescence switch mechanism to detect dimethoate. The fluorescence enhancement can sensitively reveal the existence and quantity of dimethoate.

As shown in Fig. S2, the absorption spectrum of dithizone overlaps the blue emission of animal waste biomass CDs, so that the fluorescence of CDs will be rapidly quenched by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. [36] The change in fluorescence lifetime after adding dithizone also proved that the existence of FRET, because FRET would result in a significant change of fluorescence lifetime [37]. In addition, we can clearly see that the absorption spectrum of the dimethoate (350−600 nm) did not overlap with the emission spectrum of the animal waste biomass CDs, which is thus expected to close the pathway of FRET. Consequently, the fluorescence was restored after adding dimethoate.

The influence of interfering compounds on dimethoate detection was tested to study the selectivity of the fluorescence probe. Some common interfering substances such as anions, cations, amino acid and pesticides were studied. As shown in Fig.S3, most coexisting compounds have not significant interference except Zn2+ and Pb2+. Only Zn2+ and Pb2+ at the concentration of 20 μM caused some interference with the detection of dimethoate. In order to improve the precision of the fluorescent probe, dimercaprol can be utilized as masking agent to eliminate the interference of Zn2+ and Pb2+ in water. To better understand the selectivity of fluorescent probe system, ethoprophos and profenofos which belong to organophosphorus pesticides were added, and they had no effect on the detection of dimethoate. Ethoprophos and profenofos own the PO bond, dimethoate has the PS bond. It suggests that the PS bond, but not the PO bond, plays an important role to form a stable complex on animal waste biomass CDs

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in environmental water and the comparison with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of method was evaluated by detecting dimethoate in farm water and river water. No dimethoate at detectable levels were found in the farm water and river water. The recoveries were calculated by spiking dimethoate into practical samples at 0.15, 1.0, 5.0 μM, respectively. The recoveries were in the range of 93 %–105 % and the RSDs ranging from 2% to 6%. The results show that this method is suitable for the determination of dimethoate in different water samples.

In addition, the proposed method was also compared with other approaches for detecting dimethoate (Table 1 ). The detection limit, RSDs and recoveries of this method are similar to those of other detection approaches. But the established approach can improve the selectivity, and has uncomplicated sample pretreatment. Moreover, fluorescence technology has particular merits including rapidity, low consumption and so on.

In summary, the CDs derived from pork rib bones were prepared via a simple and efficient approach with superior optical performances. We also have established a fluorescent switch process, the principle of which usually depends on the connection between a receptor unit (dithizone) and animal waste biomass CDs due to the coordination effect. After adding dimethoate, the quenching mechanism was inhibited, and then the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced. The prepared fluorescence sensor can realize fast sensitive detection of dimethoate and has the merits of high selectivity and sensitivity. In addition, the fabrication of fluorescent probe does not require enzymes, antibodies or complex surface modification, so it is inexpensive and easy to operate. Although the proposed approach is effective and convenient, there are also many challenges such as the complexity of the samples. But in general, the method offers a novel strategy to detect dimethoate pesticide residue in environmental water and expected to allow application for extensive detection of biological and organic molecules.
Haochi Liu: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jie Ding: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Ligang Chen: Writing - review & editing. Lan Ding: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Development Program of the Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (No. 20180201011GX) and Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (No. QAK201809).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112724.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly-sensitive organophosphorous pesticide biosensors based on nanostructured films of acetylcholinesterase and CdTe quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zheng, Y. Zhou, X. Li, S. Liu, Z. Tang, Highly-sensitive organophosphorous pesticide biosensors based on nanostructured films of acetylcholinesterase and CdTe quantum dots, Biosens. Bioelectron., 26 (2011) 3081-3085.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inkjet-assisted layer-by-layer printing of quantum dot/enzyme microarrays for highly sensitive detection of organophosphorous pesticides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Luan, Z. Zheng, X. Li, H. Gu, S. Liu, Inkjet-assisted layer-by-layer printing of quantum dot/enzyme microarrays for highly sensitive detection of organophosphorous pesticides, Anal. Chim. Acta, 916 (2016) 77-83.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disruption of steroidogenesis after dimethoate exposure and efficacy of N-acetylcysteine in rats: an old drug with new approaches
